item.title: Positive action - Don't just talk about love, do something
item.description: Someone is taking positive actions, big or small, in response to negative things every single day even if you don't hear or see them. "Thank you" to all you positive action people. Cheers!

item.title: Bottom shelf nightmare dump.
item.description:
@HileyNoteson Yoooooooooooooooooooooo
I don't think Imgur would like the original? I'll post the uncensored version in the comments.
You can catch me live on twitch.tv/bottomshelfvodka streaming with my favorite Imgurians from the Bottom Shelf Community server.
Join for the games! We've been playing Lethal Company, Red Dead Redemption 2, Pay Day 3, GTA V, Jackbox, Among Us, 7 Days to Die, Sea of Thieves, Barotrauma, Minecraft, Ultimate Chicken Horse...if there's a specific game you like, I guarantee you'll find a shitposting meme loving Imgurian there to play it with you. Join for the memes! Imgurians share memes in the meme dumpster 24/7 Join for the Awe! Pay your pet taxes in the pet tex channel Join for the NSFW memes! Join for the art studio and meet some amazing Imgurian artists! Join to chat with hilarious people! Join to lurk in the corners! Join for the shockingly magnificent balance of wholesomeness and chaos. discord.gg/bottomshelfvodka Still unsure about the server? Check out our museum of stupidity. https://imgur.com/gallery/FYpqqAf
Join for the meme challenges in the meme-cage-match channel
Deaf cat Magoo (short for Oogala Magoogala) tax. (Here's my actual #art dump for those interested https://imgur.com/gallery/ZaekQkF )
item.dc:creator: BottomShelfVodka

item.title: I Don’t Think This About C02 Emmisions

item.title: Thanks mom, you're such a dick
item.description: TLDR; It's not really her fault. She was (is) bipolar. Just here to vent into the void, because getting angry at her accomplishes nothing. I was a teenager and the HPV vaccine was pretty new. I was a boarder at school. My school offered all the girls the option to get it. I chose yes. I was pretty sure I wanted to have lots of sex in my life, and as little cancer as possible. I thought about it very little. Vaccinations seemed very normal to me. I thought anyone who wasn't getting it was an idiot and a prude. Somehow, my mom found out. Maybe they needed her consent? I don't know, but she unleashed full rage on the school when she found out about it. How could they let a teenager make a decision like this?? Next thing I know, I'm not getting the HPV vaccine. I thought my mom was being crazy, but this was par for the course. So, I carried on my life. Now I'm 32, I've got HPV and not a good strain. After LEEP, the lesions came back, lots of them and very fast. Everything points to cervical cancer -- not later, or in a couple years -- NOW. So I'm getting a total hysterectomy. Now. Good thing we caught it in time. Good thing I'm married. Good thing my husband didn't really want kids. Good thing we've got enough money. I feel very grateful for a lot of things. Just super super very fucking pissed at my mom. WOULD HAVE BEEN NICE TO CHOOSE NOT HAVE KIDS RATHER THAN HAVING THE THREAT OF CERVICAL CANCER MAKE THE DECISION FOR ME. NOT TO MENTION THAT I AM UNDERGOING A DANGEROUS MEDICAL PROCEDURE. AND KICKER THAT I HAVE A PANIC ATTACK EVERY TIME I *SEE* A NEEDLE OR AN IV. In fairness, my mom was a paranoid bipolar, grieving, doing her best, which was often terrible, but what's the point of being mad at her for something she likely doesn't even remember. Also, I now understand that the media is full of terrifying sensational nonsense, which I can imagine she must find difficult to navigate since her brain tells her terrifying sensational nonsense all by itself. JUST WISH I COULD KICK HER IN THE FACE RIGHT NOW. Or shout at her, "you awful fucking cow, I am 32 and how you are STILL HURTING ME" Anyway, into the void my sad story goes. Lesson of this story kids is please vaccinate your children and vaccinate your girls for HPV. Also living with someone who has a mental illness is really hard. Silver lining, no more periods. Yay? *Most viral edit: Thank you all for your kind words and support. I'm awake after my surgery and my husband is here to take care of me.
item.dc:creator: schmitschmat

item.title: Lord of the Rings Wholesome (meme) dump
item.description:
1. Awww
2
3. He must be stopped!
4
5. Ask why Aragorn is overacting when kicks the helmet?
6
7. I get that playing golf might be fun, but I will never understand people that are WATCHING it. Also how come the prize money are so huge?
8
9
10
11
12
13
14. You should have said it!
15. And finally I would like to share with this awesome community my passion project. Me and my brother have started working games in the past few years. From scratch basically and we learned how to do it on our own. Today I'm proud to say our third game is close to release. It's inspired by the "The Stanley Parable" (same kinda of style and humor) and it would mean the world to us if you wishlisted the game on Steam. Check out the page here: https://store.steampowered.com/app/2336120/Do_Not_Press_The_Button_To_Delete_The_Multiverse/ Also we finally have a voice actor to narrate the game! : ) I wish you all success in your personal passion projects! It's a blast to work on one :)))

item.title: The Rent Is Too D@mn High!
item.description: Homes are less affordable now than during the Great Depression. During the Great Depression, the median annual income was 22% of the cost of an average home. In 2022 it was 14% and it's only gotten worse in 2024. https://finance.yahoo.com/news/average-income-earners-dont-enough-200912961.html?guccounter=1

item.title: Can you imagine
item.description: The amateur radio enthusiast, known only by his call sign, managed to establish a connection with the orbiting laboratory using a combination of skill, patience, and a DIY antenna. The International Space Station is equipped with an amateur radio station that allows astronauts to communicate with people on Earth. This program, known as ARISS (Amateur Radio on the International Space Station), has been in operation for many years and provides an opportunity for students, educators, and amateur radio operators to connect with astronauts in space. To make contact with the ISS, the man built a Yagi-Uda antenna, also known as a “beam” antenna, from scratch. This type of antenna is highly directional and can be used to transmit and receive radio signals over long distances. He then carefully calculated the orbit of the ISS and waited for the right moment to make his call.
item.dc:creator: alwaysbrushyourteeth

item.title: Animation in the 40s
item.description: Drawing animations in the 1940s was an arduous process which took sometimes close to a hundred artists thousands of hours to create. This was a for a few reasons, the first is that animations such as Popeye were detailed enough that making the entire picture over again for a 10 second clip could take an individual artist tens of hours.
item.dc:creator: alwaysbrushyourteeth

item.title: Never Too Old: Elmo & ‘Sesame Street’ Still There to Help Us ... 'I gotta be honest Elmo, I’m not doing too hot,' one X user told the Muppet ...
item.description: “Elmo is just checking in! How is everybody doing?” read a post Monday on the official X, formerly Twitter, account for Sesame Street’s furry red monster. Not many are doing so great right now, according to the replies. The post garnered more than 27,000 quotes on X, with most responses airing grievances about the current state of the world. “I gotta be honest elmo, i’m not doing too hot,” one user responded. “I’m going to [expletive] lose it soon elmo!!” another wrote. “I don’t know how to answer that without traumatizing you Elmo,” added another. "Wow! Elmo is glad he asked!" the Muppet tweeted Tuesday. "Elmo learned that it is important to ask a friend how they are doing. Elmo will check in again soon, friends! Elmo loves you." On Monday afternoon, the official account for Sesame Street intervened, offering mental health resources from its nonprofit, Sesame Workshop.
item.dc:creator: Jbelkin

item.title: It’s the unhinged XL Wednesday CeleryDump for the good & bad people of Imgur
item.description:
greetings online friends
hip young principal
good times
don’t look
everyone hates on self checkout but I like it
looks cool, but I don’t think I had these
mmm hmm
relatable
perhaps
does anyone actually see 'paint store'?
if it’s licorice, no thanks
dang
depends on the type of ceiling lamp I guess
the event of a lifetime
canister- like in The Rock
this pretzel crazy
I didn’t censor that
that’s my time, have a great night gang!!

item.dc:creator: countervail247365

item.title: Orc languages – Orkish, the Black Speech and Westron
item.description:
This is a lore account on the Orcs' various languages and their history through the Ages. TL;DR: Orcs had no coherent language but many local jargons, called Orkish speech. Sauron made the Black Speech as a lingua franca in the 2nd Age but it failed. In the 3rd Age most Orcs spoke Westron [Common Speech], at least between tribes. A debased form of Black Speech appeared at the Age's end.
"The Orcs were first bred by the Dark Power of the North in the Elder Days." – The Return of the King; Appendix F onwards (art by Rick Ritchie) The Orcs were created in the First Age by Morgoth, the original Dark Lord.
"It is said that they had no language of their own, but took what they could of other tongues and perverted it to their own liking; yet they made only brutal jargons, scarcely sufficient even for their own needs, unless it were for curses and abuse."
"And these creatures, being filled with malice, hating even their own kind, quickly developed as many barbarous dialects as there were groups or settlements of their race, so that their Orkish speech was of little use to them in intercourse between different tribes."
Here may be noted to avoid confusion ahead that Orcs and Goblins in Tolkien's books are merely two different names in different languages for one race (despite how they are differentiated in other media): "Orc is not an English word. It occurs in one or two places but is usually translated goblin..." – The Hobbit; Author's Note (art by Turner Mohan)
It is possible that under the reign of Morgoth the Goblins were so concentrated in his realm in the far North that the lack of a coherent language never grew into an issue. But the First Age ended with the defeat of Morgoth, and the Northwest of Middle-earth was broken and sank into the sea.
Surviving Orcs fled towards the southeast in overwhelming force, seeking new homes (The History of Middle-earth 12; II; X): "They were now masterless and without any general leadership, but they were well-armed and very numerous, cruel, savage, and reckless in assault."
They conquered territories that belonged to Dwarves and Men, and settled in lands far and wide. To what degree the Orcs were sundered by speech already is not known, but to spread out must have ensured and hastened the confusion of tongues that soon was apperent to the second Dark Lord (art by ncorva).
Five centuries into the Second Age emerged Sauron, Morgoth's former right hand, as the new Dark Lord. From that on Sauron brought the Orcs under his sway but he inherited from the Elder Days "difficulties, such as the diversity of the Orcs in breed and language, and the feuds among them" (HoME 10; V; X).
The Goblins in many places had "set up petty realms of their own and had become accustomed to independence. Nonetheless Sauron in time managed to unite them all in unreasoning hatred of the Elves and of Men who associated with them" (ibid). Still he had the practical issue of communication among his various subjects at hand, not least the incompatible kinds of Orkish speech.
About the middle of the Second Age he aimed to amend the problem: "It is said that the Black Speech was devised by Sauron in the Dark Years, and that he had desired to make it the language of all those that served him, but he failed in that purpose." – RotK; Appendix F (art by @galerifoton)
It existed no later than about year 1600 of the Second Age, when Sauron made the One Ring. On it he wrote with the High Elves' alphabet called Tengwar, "for they have no letters in Mordor for such subtle work", but the language was that of Mordor (The Fellowship of the Ring; II; II).
Few Black Speech words are known and the only sentence in the ancient form is the Ring-verse (ibid): "Ash nazg durbatulûk, ash nazg gimbatul, ash nazg thrakatulûk agh burzum-ishi krimpatul" "One Ring to rule them all, One Ring to find them, One Ring to bring them all and in the Darkness bind them"
The Black Speech was made to be distinct from Elvish and "other real languages that are most familiar: English, Latin, Greek, etc" (Letter 297). But the word nazg ["ring"] Tolkien considered might unconsciously been inspired by nasc in Irish Gaelic (nasg in Scottish Gaelic) that also means ring.
The Professor described the language that he created for Mordor (art by DanikYaroslavTomyn): "The Black Speech was not intentionally modeled on any style, but was meant to be self consistent, very different from Elvish, yet organized and expressive, as would be expected of a device of Sauron before his complete corruption. It was evidently an agglutinative language." – Parma Eldalemberon 17; Words, Phrases and Passages in Various Tongues in The Lord of the Rings
"I have tried to play fair linguistically, and it is meant to have a meaning not be a mere casual group of nasty noises..." (ibid). As a lover of sound Tolkien had no appreciation for the Black Speech. He wrote, in Letter 343, after "a drinking goblet arrived (from a fan) which proved to be of steel engraved with the terrible words seen on the Ring. I of course have never drunk from it, but use it for tobacco ash."
In the late half of the Second Age began Dúnedain from the island of Númenor to settle in Middle-earth, notably about the "Mouths of Anduin, and it is said that it was the language of that region (which was afterwards called Gondor) that was the foundation of the Common Speech" (HoME 12; I; II). The tongue of Númenor mixed with those of native Men and resulted in Westron. It spread across the Northwest and became a lingua franca among Men, Dwarves and Elves [and it was later adopted by the Hobbits]. For this reason became Westron known as the Common Speech (art by Jason Carter).
"But Sauron, who could turn all things devised by Elves or Men to his own evil purposes, had also favoured the spread of this Common Speech, for it was useful to him in the governing of his vast lordship in the Dark Years." –ibid The exact significance of this is not clear. It is possible that it was a discarded idea, but it aligns both with the Orcs later linguistic history and the Black Speech's failure.
My understanding is that Sauron at some point at large gave up on imposing Black Speech among his servants in favour of Westron. It is also perfectly possible that the Dark Lord in the Second Age utilized both languages in parallel, depending on where it proved most profitable to his designs. Tolkien wrote "The Black Speech was only used in Mordor.... It was never used willingly by any other people..." (Letter 144); the former likely only concerns the end of the Third Age [more on that later] but the latter may applied to the Second Age too. If so it is clear why the lingua franca failed: no one liked it.
Letter 144 also reminds us that the names known from Sauron's land are exonyms [names used by people of other places and languages]. They are either given in Westron [that is translated as English] or in Elvish. For example, the name Mordor is in Elven Sindarin and means the "Black Land". Another example is the Black Gate, known as Morannon in Sindarin and of the same meaning, yet we do not know the fortification's name in the Black Speech. The only exception is the Dark Tower, Barad-dûr, that was named Lugbúrz ["Tower-dark"] in Sauron's own speech (so of same meaning, but still).
Whether Sauron lost faith in his constructed language or not, in the 35th century was he defeated in the War of the Last Alliance, and with his apperent demise ended the Second Age (art by David Wiles). The Black Speech did not last as a living language; after Sauron's fall "this language in its ancient form was forgotten by all but the Nazgûl" (RotK; Appendix F onwards).
Instead "in the Third Age Orcs used for communication between breed and breed the Westron tongue; and many indeed of the older tribes, such as those that still lingered in the North and in the Misty Mountains, had long used the Westron as their native language, though in such a fashion as to make it hardly less unlovely than Orkish" (art by MissSwarm).
"In this jargon tark, 'man of Gondor', was a debased form of tarkil, a Quenya word used in Westron for one of Númenorean descent" (ibid). This note was made in reference to an experience Samwise had. When the Orcs and Uruks in the tower of Cirith Ungol were alerted to an intruder, not knowing it was merely Samwise, a small Orc was afraid that the intruder was "one of the filthy tarks" (RotK; VI; I).
Hobbits also adopted Westron as mother tongue and this was why in The Hobbit and The Lord of the Rings, at the end of the Third Age, the halflings often understood what Orcs said (art by John Howe). An example of Westron used for communication between different groups is from when Merry and Pippin were held as prisoners by a company that consisted of Uruks from Isengard, Orcs from Mordor, and Orcs from the Misty Mountains;
An Orc adressed Pippin "in the Common Speech, which he made almost as hideous as his own language.... When To Pippin's surprise he found that much of the talk was intelligible many of the Orcs were using ordinary language. Apparently the members of two or three quite different tribes were present, and they could not understand one another's orc-speech." – TTT; III; III (art by John Howe)
Now and then Tolkien wrote lines of Orcs speaking crude Westron [translated as English] yet it was still written out clearer than it was to other peoples who had to listen to their jargon: "...Orcs and Trolls spoke as they would, without love of words or things; and their language was actually more degraded and filthy than I have shown it I do not suppose that any will wish for a closer rendering, though models are easy to find."
Sauron's attempt to provide a lingua franca had still left traces in goblin vocabulary across Middle-earth (RotK; Appendix F): "From the Black Speech, however, were derived many of the words that were in the Third Age widespread among the Orcs, such as ghâsh 'fire'..."
Gandalf heard Orcs speak when the Fellowship was in Moria. He told his companions (The Fellowship of the Ring; II; V, art by Donato Giancola): "...they seemed to be talking in their own hideous language. All I caught was ghâsh; that is "fire"."
Despite all got Black Speech a short renaissance at the end of the Third Age. A decade after The Hobbit, in year 2951, "Sauron declares himself openly and gathers power in Mordor. He begins the rebuilding of Barad-dûr" (RotK; Appendix B, art by Bastien Grivet and Jessica Rossier).
Then the Black Speech "became once more the language of Barad-dûr and of the captains of Mordor" (RotK; Appendix F). Yet if anyone learned the ancient Black Speech is unclear, as we only read about a "debased form used by the soldiers of the Dark Tower..." It is also unknown how far the ancient and the debased versions diverged from one another (art by Steve Firchow).
Among those who held Merry and Pippin captive were as mentioned some Mordor-orcs. They were not on friendly terms with the Uruks from Isengard, who were led by Uglúk and served Saruman. An unnamed Mordor-orc cursed "Uglúk u bagronk sha pushdug Saruman-glob búbhosh skai'" (TTT; III; III). This is our only known sentence beside the Ring-verse but is in debased speech. It means "Uglúk to the cesspool, sha! the dungfilth; the great Saruman-fool, skai!" (HoME 12; I; II; Note on an unpublished letter; Note 6) or "Uglúk to the dung-pit with stinking Saruman-filth, pig-guts, gah!" ( Vinyar Tengwar 26; UttD).
Christopher Tolkien found a third translation of the curse given by his father (HoME 12; Foreword; Note). "All three differ significantly (bagronk, for example, being rendered both as 'cesspool' and as 'torture (chamber)'); from which it seems clear that my father was at this time devising interpretations of the words, whatever he may have intended them to mean when he first wrote them."
At least one word from debased Black Speech came into use among Men. "Sharkû in that tongue means old man" (RotK; Appendix F), and it was presumably used by Orcs for Saruman, because human ruffians who also served Saruman referred to him as "Sharkey" (RotK; VI; VIII, art by Donato Giancola). One word in ancient Black Speech was however well-known among the Free Peoples: Nazgûl. It means "ringwraith(s)" as it is both a singular and a plural term.
One could also argue Uruk from Black Speech, though it derived from Elven words with the root Ruku, that in Elven speech became for example Orco and Orch. From Elves it came to other peoples, such as Urku on Númenor and Orka in Westron, while in Dwarven speech it was rendered Rukhs. Technically Uruk means Orc but it "was applied as a rule only to the great soldier-orcs that at this time issued from Mordor and Isengard. The lesser kinds were called, especially by the Uruk-hai, snaga 'slave'" (RotK; Appendix F). Hai means "folk" so Uruk-hai simply means "Orc-folk".
Apart from the verse on the One Ring was pure Black Speech presumably spoken once in the LotR, though unfortunately it is not written out. At Minas Tirith was the attackers' huge battering ram enchanted by the Witch-king, the Lord of the Nazgûl (RotK; V; IV, art by ralphdamiani): "Then the Black Captain rose in his stirrups and cried aloud in a dreadful voice, speaking in some forgotten tongue words of power and terror to rend both heart and stone."
Concerning writing, the runes known as Cirth had Elven origin but while Elves turned to Tengwar letters the Cirth in the Second Age spread "to many peoples, to Men and Dwarves, and even to Orcs, all of whom altered them to suit their purposes and according to their skill or lack of it" (RotK; Appendix E). Cirth ended up so associated with Dwarves that it became known "as 'dwarf-letters'" (HoME 12; II; X); and in the late Third age the Orc leader Azog wrote his name "in Dwarf-runes" (RotK; Appendix A; III). For Orcs to write Westron or even Orkish with crude Cirth is thus plausible. But what about the Black Speech?
Only on the One Ring was Elven Tengwar used to write Black Speech, while concerning a rune on Orc gear we learn that "Sauron does not use the Elf-runes" (TTT; III; I). From this is seems clear neither Tengwar or any form of Cirth were formally used to write Black Speech [as Tengwar had letters, not runes]. Near Mordor "Orcs and other foul servants" of Sauron carved "evil runes or the fell sign of the Eye" into trees (TTT; IV; IV). Were these original runes deviced for Mordor? Or merely a form of Cirth runes not in themselves evil but used to write evil messages (in Black Speech?), not carved on Sauron's orders? We may hope that new sources will come to light to answer the matter ahead (art by @jonnyhodgsonart).
In 3019 occured the final defeat of Sauron, through the destruction of the One Ring, and two years later ended the Third Age (art by Ted Nasmith). We do not know anything about Orc languages in the following Age(s), but thinking of Letter 144 a qualified speculation is that the revived Black Speech did not long would survive its maker. Presumably Orcs instead stuck to defile Westron and curse in Orkish speech to their hearts' delight.
For this account I have had help from the work by linguist and Tolkien scholar Helge Kåre Fauskanger. For Tolkien's varied languages I often refer to his Ardalambion: https://folk.uib.no/hnohf/index.html His article on Orkish and the Black Speech with grammar, a word list and an interesting thesis that the Black Speech was inspired by Hittite/Hurrian: https://folk.uib.no/hnohf/o2.htm His article on the Common Speech/Westron with grammar and a word list: https://folk.uib.no/hnohf/westron.htm
— — — — — For more of my Tolkien lore posts, enjoy these two indexes: Character bios https://imgur.com/gallery/axV2mPF Folks, animals, items, etc https://imgur.com/gallery/AQK9pJL
item.dc:creator: Ngugi

item.title: Looking for the best order of panel for best result
item.description: Hello Grylock here I m finishing this strip, but I'm unsure about the best panel order. This is the original layout. But i m thinking 3-4-2-1 Or 2-1-3-4 Works as well. Edit: Hey Thanks for the front page :) The final version will come later this week I still wanted to animate one of the panels. A first for me. For more of my comics, the best place is still Instagram. https://www.instagram.com/gryzlock_comics Cheers
item.dc:creator: gryzlock

item.title: Scrap The Cap
